questions.question: What was the name of Napoleon Bonaparte's final battle?
questions.explanation: The Battle of Waterloo was fought on June 18, 1815, near Brussels, Belgium. Napoleon's French forces were defeated by the Duke of Wellington's allied army and Prussian forces under Blücher. Napoleon was exiled to Saint Helena, where he died in 1821. The phrase 'met his Waterloo' derives from this battle.
questions.memory_hook: Waterloo = Napoleon's end. ABBA even made a pop song about surrender.

questions.question: Which organization was established after World War II to promote international peace and security?
questions.explanation: The United Nations was established on October 24, 1945, replacing the failed League of Nations. It was founded by 51 countries and now has 193 member states. Its main purpose is to maintain international peace, develop friendly relations among nations, and promote social progress.
questions.memory_hook: UN after WWII — the League of Nations failed once, so they tried again.

questions.question: What is the official length of a standard Olympic swimming pool in meters?
questions.explanation: An Olympic swimming pool is 50 meters long and 25 meters wide, with eight lanes each 2.5 meters wide. Short-course competitions use 25-meter pools. World records set in 50-meter pools are the official 'long-course' records and are considered the definitive world standards.
questions.memory_hook: 50m pool, 8 lanes — the Olympic standard. Short course is half that.

questions.question: In darts, what is the highest score achievable with a single dart?
questions.explanation: The highest score with a single dart is 60, achieved by hitting the triple 20 (the small segment in the triple-scoring ring on the 20 section). The bullseye is worth 50 points. A 'perfect leg' in professional darts requires nine darts — three treble 20s (180), two more 180s (360), then finishing on 141.
questions.memory_hook: Triple 20 = 60. Top of the board, triple ring, maximum dart score.

questions.question: What is the name of the island in 'Cast Away' where the FedEx plane crashes?
questions.explanation: In Cast Away (2000) starring Tom Hanks, the island where he is stranded is never given a name in the film. The movie was filmed primarily on the Monuriki island in Fiji. Wilson the volleyball became his most famous companion. The film explored isolation and the will to survive.
questions.memory_hook: Cast Away's island has no name — it's just Tom Hanks, Wilson, and the ocean.

questions.question: Which rapper's real name is Shawn Corey Carter?
questions.explanation: Jay-Z's real name is Shawn Corey Carter. He was born in Brooklyn, New York, and is one of the best-selling music artists of all time with over 100 million records sold. He co-founded Roc-A-Fella Records and Roc Nation, and is married to Beyoncé. He's also the first rapper inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame.
questions.memory_hook: Shawn Carter became Jay-Z — from Marcy Projects to global mogul.

questions.question: Ben-Hur (1959) was the first film to win 11 Academy Awards — a record later tied by which two films?
options: Titanic and The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King
questions.answer: Titanic and The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King
questions.explanation: Ben-Hur (1959) was the first film to win 11 Academy Awards. The record was later tied by Titanic (1997) and The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King (2003), which also won all 11 of its nominations. No film has ever won more than 11 Oscars.
questions.memory_hook: 11 wins, three times — Ben-Hur, Titanic, Return of the King.

questions.question: Who was the first female Prime Minister of the United Kingdom?
questions.explanation: Margaret Thatcher became the UK's first female Prime Minister on May 4, 1979, leading the Conservative Party. She served until 1990, becoming the longest-serving PM of the 20th century. Nicknamed 'The Iron Lady,' she implemented free-market economic policies that became known as 'Thatcherism.'
questions.memory_hook: Iron Lady, first female PM — Thatcher in 1979.

questions.question: The Dead Sea, the lowest point on Earth's surface, borders which two countries?
questions.explanation: The Dead Sea sits on the border between Israel to the west and Jordan to the east, at approximately 430 meters below sea level — the lowest point on Earth's surface. Its extreme salinity (about 34%) makes it nearly impossible for organisms to survive in it, which is how it got its name.
questions.memory_hook: Dead Sea = Israel and Jordan. So salty nothing lives in it. So far below sea level that the water can't drain anywhere. You float effortlessly — the salt forces you up.

questions.question: What force keeps planets in orbit around the Sun?
questions.explanation: Gravity is the attractive force between masses. The Sun's enormous mass creates a gravitational field that keeps planets in elliptical orbits. Without gravity, planets would travel in a straight line and fly off into space. Newton first described gravity mathematically; Einstein later explained it as the curvature of spacetime caused by mass.
questions.memory_hook: Gravity holds the solar system together. Without it, the planets would fly off in straight lines. Newton saw an apple fall and figured out what holds the universe together.

questions.question: Mongolia is one of the world's most sparsely populated countries. What is it geographically landlocked by?
questions.explanation: Mongolia is a landlocked country bordered only by Russia to the north and China to the south, east, and west. With an area of 1.56 million km² and a population of only about 3.3 million people, it has the world's lowest population density among sovereign nations (about 2 people per km²). More than half the population lives in Ulaanbaatar, the world's coldest capital city. The Gobi Desert covers the southern third of the country.
questions.memory_hook: Mongolia: landlocked between Russia and China only. 1.56M km² with only 3.3M people — lowest population density of any sovereign nation. Ulaanbaatar = world's coldest capital city. Gobi Desert covers the south. Home of the world's largest empire (Mongol).

questions.question: Quasars, first identified in the 1960s, are the most luminous persistent objects in the observable universe — some outshine entire galaxies by a factor of 1,000. What are they?
options: Quasars are extremely massive stars in the early universe that burned so brightly they fused elements all the way up to uranium, creating intense radiation before collapsing into black holes

Quasars are the intensely luminous cores of distant galaxies powered by supermassive black holes actively consuming surrounding gas and dust — the infalling material releases enormous energy as it heats and spirals inward
Quasars are chains of interconnected galaxies whose combined gravitational energy generates a coherent beam of radiation visible across the observable universe
Quasars are remnant shockwaves from galaxy collisions — when two galaxies merge, the combined gravitational energy is released as electromagnetic radiation that can last for millions of years
questions.answer: Quasars are the intensely luminous cores of distant galaxies powered by supermassive black holes actively consuming surrounding gas and dust — the infalling material releases enormous energy as it heats and spirals inward
questions.explanation: The name 'quasar' is short for 'quasi-stellar radio source' — they appeared as point-like stars but with inexplicably large redshifts, meaning they were enormously distant. The energy mechanism was cracked when astronomers realized the only known process that could generate such luminosity from such a small region was a supermassive black hole actively accreting matter. Gas spiraling into the black hole heats to millions of degrees and radiates across the electromagnetic spectrum. Most galaxies, including the Milky Way, likely hosted quasars in their youth — our central black hole (Sagittarius A*) is dormant.
questions.memory_hook: Quasars stumped astronomers in the 1960s — they looked like stars but had redshifts implying they were billions of light-years away. The source of their impossibly bright energy: supermassive black holes eating gas. Our own galaxy's black hole (Sagittarius A*) was a quasar billions of years ago.
